health_association.scientific: Measures how health-associated your species composition is based on the GMWI2 model of 155 taxa.
health_association.non_expert: This shows how many of your bacteria are the types typically found in healthy people — think of it as your gut's health fingerprint.

diversity_resilience.scientific: Combined Shannon diversity and guild-level evenness — measures species richness and structural redundancy.
diversity_resilience.non_expert: How many different types of bacteria you have and how well-balanced they are — more variety means your gut can handle stress and recover faster.

metabolic_function.scientific: Composite of 4 CLR diagnostic ratios (CUR, FCR, MDR, PPR) measuring substrate utilization, fermentation efficiency, mucus dependency, and putrefaction pressure.
metabolic_function.non_expert: This shows how well your bacteria are processing food — are they running on the right fuel, converting it efficiently, and producing gentle rather than harsh byproducts.

guild_balance.scientific: Evaluates 6 functional guilds by abundance position within reference ranges and CLR competitive status, weighted by ecological dependency.
guild_balance.non_expert: This checks whether your six bacterial teams are properly staffed — each team has a job, and this score shows if they have enough members to do their work well.

safety_profile.scientific: Absence of dysbiosis-associated taxa (F. nucleatum, S. gallolyticus, P. anaerobius, E-S), extreme guild overgrowth, and BCFA pathway activity.
safety_profile.non_expert: This checks for any red flags — harmful bacteria, extreme overgrowth, or signs of unhealthy fermentation. A high score here means your gut has a clean safety record.

score_drivers.key_note: Your strongest area is metabolic function (100% of its potential)

score_summary: Your overall score is 85.7 out of 100. The main area to focus on is fiber processing.

Cross-Feeders.client_interpretation: Your connector bacteria are thriving at higher-than-typical levels, which suggests excellent communication between different bacterial teams in your gut. This creates efficient metabolic handoffs that support overall digestive harmony.

Fiber Degraders.client_interpretation: Your fiber-processing bacteria are running below optimal levels, though they appear stable and balanced. Expanding this foundational team would help maximize your gut's ability to extract nutrients and beneficial compounds from plant materials.

Butyrate Producers.client_interpretation: Your butyrate-producing bacteria are in excellent shape, creating the right amount of this important fuel that nourishes your gut lining and supports intestinal health.

Bifidobacteria.client_interpretation: Your Bifidobacteria are flourishing at optimal levels with exceptional resilience, providing strong foundational support for immune function and creating an environment that favors beneficial bacterial growth.

Proteolytic Guild.client_interpretation: Your protein-fermenting bacteria are at healthy levels but may be experiencing some competitive pressure from other bacterial groups. This balanced state helps prevent excessive protein fermentation while maintaining necessary functions.

Mucin Degraders.client_interpretation: Your mucus-layer bacteria are within normal levels but appear to be under competitive pressure from other bacterial groups. Despite this challenge, they maintain good resilience and continue supporting your protective gut barrier.

strengths.title: Low inflammatory pressure
strengths.text: Your beneficial bacteria are producing mostly gentle, protective short-chain fatty acids with minimal harsh protein-fermentation byproducts. This is an important marker of gut health.

strengths.title: Good diversity
strengths.text: Your gut shows moderate-to-good species diversity (Shannon 3.20). A varied bacterial community provides a more stable and adaptable foundation for gut health.

strengths.title: Strong butyrate support
strengths.text: Your butyrate-producing bacteria are at 14.2% — within the healthy 10-25% range. These bacteria produce the primary fuel for your gut lining cells, supporting barrier integrity and reducing inflammation.

strengths.title: Abundant bifidobacteria
strengths.text: Your Bifidobacteria are thriving at 10.3%, above the typical reference range. Strong Bifidobacteria presence supports immune function and helps amplify beneficial short-chain fatty acid production.

strengths.title: Balanced guild relationships
strengths.text: Most of your bacterial groups are within their healthy ranges with no extreme competitive imbalances. This reflects a well-organised gut ecosystem where different microbial teams are coexisting in proportion.

strengths.title: Strong gut lining support
strengths.text: Your gut lining has good protection: butyrate producers are active, your bacteria are fed primarily by dietary fiber rather than mucus, and mucin degradation is controlled. This combination supports a well-maintained gut barrier.

challenges.title: Fiber-processing bacteria below range
challenges.text: Your fiber-processing bacteria are at 15.2% — below their healthy range of 30-50%. This is the entry point for the entire fermentation chain, so supporting this group is a primary target.

all_strengths.title: Clean safety profile
all_strengths.text: None of the key dysbiosis-associated bacteria (Fusobacterium nucleatum, Streptococcus gallolyticus, Peptostreptococcus anaerobius, Escherichia-Shigella) were detected at concerning levels. Your gut has a reassuring safety baseline.

all_strengths.title: Diet-fed ecosystem
all_strengths.text: Your bacteria are primarily fuelled by what you eat rather than by eroding your gut's protective mucus layer. This is the ecologically stable pattern and reflects a gut that has adequate dietary substrate.

all_strengths.title: Efficient fermentation
all_strengths.text: Your gut's fermentation assembly line is running well — intermediate fermentation compounds are being fully converted into beneficial short-chain fatty acids rather than accumulating. This means your bacteria are cooperating efficiently.

all_challenges.title: Fiber-processing bacteria below range
all_challenges.text: Your fiber-processing bacteria are at 15.2% — below their healthy range of 30-50%. This is the entry point for the entire fermentation chain, so supporting this group is a primary target.

strengths_challenges.bottom_line: The main issue is not total collapse — it is imbalance in a few high-impact areas. Your gut still has strong foundations to build on.

good_news: Your gut bacteria community is well-balanced and stable, with strong connector bacteria that help different teams work together smoothly during daily changes.

deviation.description: Your Fiber-Processing Bacteria are at 15.2% — below the healthy range of 30–50%.

deviation_cards.narrative: Your fiber-processing bacteria are the workhorses of your gut — they break down the fiber from fruits, vegetables, and whole grains into protective compounds that keep your gut lining healthy and support your immune system. At 15.2%, you have about half of what we'd expect to see in a thriving gut ecosystem. This matters for your day-to-day health because these bacteria produce the compounds that help regulate inflammation, support your mood through the gut-brain connection, and strengthen your immune defenses. When their numbers are low, you might notice that your body doesn't bounce back from stress as easily, and your overall resilience feels diminished. The good news is that these bacteria respond beautifully to the right support — they're just waiting for the right conditions to flourish again.
deviation_cards.summary_line: Your fiber-processing bacteria took a hit from recent antibiotics and lifestyle stressors, but they're incredibly responsive to targeted support and will rebuild with the right approach.
deviation_cards.health_meaning: Your Fiber-Processing Bacteria are at 15.2% — below the healthy range of 30–50%.

kb_drivers.label: Antibiotic Use
kb_drivers.text: Following antibiotic use, the total protective output from your gut bacteria — including all the short-chain fatty acids that protect your lining — can fall dramatically. This effect can persist for months after the antibiotic course ends.
kb_drivers.kb_text: Following antibiotic use, the total protective output from your gut bacteria — including all the short-chain fatty acids that protect your lining — can fall dramatically. This effect can persist for months after the antibiotic course ends.

kb_drivers.label: Tobacco / Smoking
kb_drivers.text: The composition of gut bacteria differs significantly between smokers and non-smokers — smoking creates a distinct microbial environment that tends to favor less beneficial bacterial communities.
kb_drivers.kb_text: The composition of gut bacteria differs significantly between smokers and non-smokers — smoking creates a distinct microbial environment that tends to favor less beneficial bacterial communities.

kb_drivers.label: Chronic Stress & Anxiety
kb_drivers.text: Chronic stress activates your body's stress response system, which directly influences your gut environment. Under sustained stress, beneficial bacteria — particularly butyrate producers — tend to decline, reducing the gut's protective capacity.
kb_drivers.kb_text: Chronic stress activates your body's stress response system, which directly influences your gut environment. Under sustained stress, beneficial bacteria — particularly butyrate producers — tend to decline, reducing the gut's protective capacity.

awareness_chips.summary_text: Regular alcohol consumption is a documented contributor to the gut imbalance patterns we're seeing — particularly the depletion of butyrate-producing bacteria. Reducing alcohol intake is one of the most impactful lifestyle changes for gut health recovery.

Regular physical activity is one of the lifestyle factors most consistently associated with a healthier microbiome — particularly higher butyrate-producing bacteria and greater diversity. Increasing activity is a meaningful complement to your supplement protocol.

Around 70% of your immune system operates in and around your gut. When gut bacteria that produce butyrate are depleted, the physical and immunological defenses of your gut barrier are weakened — which is directly connected to increased immune vulnerability and susceptibility to infections.

root_cause_data.section_summary: Your gut is telling the story of a recent perfect storm — necessary antibiotics, ongoing stress, and occasional smoking all converged to disrupt your beneficial bacteria. The silver lining is that fiber-processing bacteria are among the most responsive to positive changes, and your daily fermented foods show you're already supporting recovery.

cited_papers.title: When smoke meets gut: deciphering the interactions between tobacco smoking and gut microbiota in disease development
cited_papers.abstract: Tobacco smoking is a prevalent and detrimental habit practiced worldwide, increasing the risk of various diseases, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), cardiovascular disease, liver disease, and cancer. Although previous research has explored the detrimental health effects of tobacco smoking, recent studies suggest that gut microbiota dysbiosis may play a critical role in these outcomes. Numerous tobacco smoke components, such as nicotine, are found in the gastrointestinal tract and interact with gut microbiota, leading to lasting impacts on host health and diseases. This review delves into the ways tobacco smoking and its various constituents influence gut microbiota composition and functionality. We also summarize recent advancements in understanding how tobacco smokin

cited_papers.title: Lactiplantibacillus plantarum Intake Reduces Gastrointestinal Tract Damage Caused by Chronic Stress in Young Rats
cited_papers.abstract: Stress is the body’s coordinated response to stimuli that threaten its internal stability. It is a risk factor that alters the functionality of the gastrointestinal tract. This study evaluated the effect of Lactiplantibacillus plantarum 299v on chronic stress-induced morphological alterations in the small and large intestine, the expression of intestinal barrier and immune related genes, and gut microbiota composition in 52-day old male Wistar rats. The animals were randomly assigned to three groups: control (C), chronic restraint stress (S), and chronic restraint stress plus L. plantarum (1 × 1010 CFU/mL, SP). The chronic restraint stress protocol consisted of a 1-h daily session, 5 days per week, for four consecutive weeks. Administration of L. plantarum increased the number of goblet ce

cited_papers.title: Gut Microbiota-Driven Pathways Linking Chronic Stress to Tumor Progression
cited_papers.abstract: Chronic stress is increasingly recognized as a critical factor influencing tumor progression, but its underlying mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This review examines the role of gut microbiota as a critical mediator linking chronic stress to tumor progression. Recent evidence suggests that chronic stress triggers gut dysbiosis, characterized by reduced microbial diversity, depletion of beneficial bacteria, and enrichment of potentially harmful species. We summarize the mechanisms by which chronic stress regulates gut microbial dysbiosis, including stress-related hormone signaling, intestinal inflammation, mucosal barrier disruption, and altered gut motility. Additionally, we examine how stress-induced dysbiosis contributes to tumor progression through immune suppression, metabol

cited_papers.title: Metagenomic analysis of the impact of nitrofurantoin treatment on the human faecal microbiota.
cited_papers.abstract:
OBJECTIVES
The objective was to study changes in the faecal microbiota of patients with uncomplicated urinary tract infections (UTIs) treated with nitrofurantoin and of non-treated healthy controls using 16S rRNA analysis.


METHODS
Serial stool samples were collected from patients receiving nitrofurantoin treatment at different timepoints [before treatment (day 1; T1), within 48 h of end of treatment (days 5-15; T2) and 28 days after treatment (days 31-43; T3)], as well as from healthy controls. Direct DNA extraction (PowerSoil DNA Isolation Kit, MoBio Laboratories, Carlsbad, CA, USA) from stool samples was followed by pyrosequencing (454 GS FLX Titanium) of the V3-V5 region of the bacterial 16S rRNA gene.


RESULTS
Among UTI patients, mean proportions of the Actinobacteria phylum increas

timeline_phases.label: Settling in
timeline_phases.title: 🌱 Settling in
timeline_phases.body: The probiotic strains in your capsule are adapting to your gut environment — encountering existing bacteria, beginning to colonise, and establishing early positions. Your daily prebiotic sachet is reaching the lower gut, priming the environment before measurable compositional shifts occur. Vitamin C, Zinc, and B-vitamins from your sachet begin supporting immune and energy pathways within days. The LP815 strain begins producing calming GABA from week 1, supporting stress-axis balance. You may notice small changes in digestion rhythm — this is normal as your gut adjusts.

timeline_phases.label: Early momentum
timeline_phases.title: 🔋 Early momentum
timeline_phases.body: Beneficial bacteria begin expanding as prebiotic fibres consistently supply the substrate they need. Your fiber degraders start moving toward healthy abundance levels. Short-chain fatty acid production increases as the fermentation chain becomes more active. Omega-3 EPA and DHA from your softgels are building up in cell membranes, typically reaching effective anti-inflammatory concentrations by week 6. Vitamin D3 supports ongoing immune regulation. Many people start noticing improvements in digestion consistency and gut comfort during this window.

timeline_phases.label: Deepening balance
timeline_phases.title: 🛡️ Deepening balance
timeline_phases.body: Guild populations move toward their target ranges. Butyrate output increases as the fermentation chain becomes more complete — your gut lining receives more of the protective compounds it needs for repair. Ashwagandha's adaptogenic effects on the stress axis compound with continued use, typically most noticeable from week 8 onward. Magnesium Bisglycinate supports deeper sleep and stress resilience as tissue saturation builds. Inflammatory metabolite pressure typically decreases during this phase as the SCFA-to-protein fermentation balance shifts.

timeline_phases.label: Consolidation
timeline_phases.title: ⚖️ Consolidation
timeline_phases.body: The ecological shifts from earlier phases become self-sustaining. A more diverse, well-balanced community is better equipped to hold its gains under normal life pressures — stress, travel, or temporary dietary changes. Botanical adaptogens and the full vitamin-mineral stack have reached their most effective tissue concentrations. Weeks 13–16 are the ideal window for a reassessment sample to measure the full compositional shift.

lifestyle_recommendations.title: Prioritize diverse fiber sources daily
lifestyle_recommendations.text: With your fiber-processing bacteria at only 15.2% (well below the 30-50% range), focus on incorporating multiple categories of plant-based fibers at each meal to rebuild this critical bacterial community.

lifestyle_recommendations.title: Gradually increase physical activity intensity
lifestyle_recommendations.text: Since you currently engage in light activity, progressively add moderate-intensity exercise sessions 3-4 times weekly to enhance beneficial gut bacteria that support both immune function and mood regulation.

lifestyle_recommendations.title: Implement daily stress-reduction practices
lifestyle_recommendations.text: Given your high stress level (7/10), incorporate 10-15 minutes of mindfulness or breathing exercises daily to support gut barrier function and optimize the stress-microbiome connection.

lifestyle_recommendations.title: Maintain consistent meal timing patterns
lifestyle_recommendations.text: Establish regular eating windows and avoid late-night meals to support your circadian rhythm and complement your excellent sleep quality (9/10) for optimal microbiome function.

lifestyle_recommendations.title: Support fiber fermentation with adequate hydration
lifestyle_recommendations.text: Increase water intake throughout the day, especially when boosting fiber consumption, to facilitate proper bacterial fermentation and short-chain fatty acid production for immune and mood benefits.

supplement_cards.name: Probiotic Hard Capsule
supplement_cards.what_it_is: Live beneficial bacteria, selected specifically for your microbiome pattern.
supplement_cards.why: Mix 3 (Fiber & SCFA Restoration) targets your fiber-processing bacteria, which sit below range at 15.2% despite being compositionally competitive. These strains provide cross-feeding support and substrate provisioning to expand your fiber degrader guild back into its functional range. LP815 adds psychobiotic GABA production for stress/mood support.

supplement_cards.name: Omega + Antioxidant Softgel
supplement_cards.what_it_is: Essential fatty acids and antioxidants in an easy-to-absorb softgel form.
supplement_cards.why: Omega-3 EPA and DHA support gut barrier integrity, reduce inflammatory signalling, and support brain and mood function. Vitamin D3 regulates immune training. Astaxanthin provides potent antioxidant protection.

supplement_cards.name: Prebiotic & Botanical Powder Jar
supplement_cards.what_it_is: A blend of prebiotic fibres mixed into water — food for your beneficial bacteria, designed for easy daily use.
supplement_cards.why: This prebiotic blend selectively feeds the beneficial bacteria identified as needing support. PHGG provides a gentle base, Beta-glucans and Resistant starch boost SCFA and butyrate production, while Inulin, FOS, and GOS are the specific substrates your probiotic strains (especially L. acidophilus NCFM) need to thrive.

supplement_cards.name: Morning Wellness Capsules
supplement_cards.what_it_is: Targeted vitamins, minerals, and polyphenol compounds selected for your specific health profile.
supplement_cards.why: Capsule 1 addresses immune resilience (Vitamin C + Zinc + Selenium) and fatigue (B12 + Folate). Capsule 2 provides potent anti-inflammatory action through Curcumin co-formulated with Piperine for 2,000% enhanced absorption — best taken with food.

supplement_cards.name: Evening Wellness Capsules
supplement_cards.what_it_is: Botanical adaptogens and mineral support timed to work with your body's evening recovery processes.
supplement_cards.why: Capsule 1: Ashwagandha is a clinically-evidenced adaptogen for cortisol reduction and anxiety relief — directly addressing your stress level (7/10) and mood improvement goal. Propolis provides natural antibacterial/antiviral support for immune resilience. Capsule 2: Magnesium Bisglycinate supports deep sleep, stress resilience, and nervous system recovery — the bisglycinate form is chosen for superior absorption and calming effect.

goal_cards.title: Stronger immune resilience
goal_cards.mechanism: Around 70% of your immune system is shaped by your gut bacteria. Beneficial bacteria modulate immune signalling, train immune cells, and maintain the gut barrier that prevents unwanted particles from triggering immune responses.
goal_cards.formula_link: Your formula targets this through Zinc and Vitamin C in the sachet (directly support immune cell activity), Omega-3 EPA/DHA (resolve inflammatory signals), Vitamin D3 (regulates immune training), and the probiotic mix (rebuilds beneficial bacteria that educate immune cells).

goal_cards.title: Better mood and reduced anxiety
goal_cards.mechanism: Your gut produces about 90% of your body's serotonin and communicates directly with your brain via the vagus nerve. Gut microbiome imbalances — especially disruptions to beneficial bacteria — have been consistently linked to mood and anxiety patterns.
goal_cards.formula_link: Your formula targets this through Ashwagandha (clinical evidence for cortisol reduction and anxiety relief), LP815 probiotic strain (produces calming GABA), Omega-3 DHA (brain health and mood regulation), and Magnesium Bisglycinate (supports the nervous system and stress resilience).

goal_cards.title: Optimise metabolism
goal_cards.mechanism: A balanced, resilient gut microbiome provides the foundation for many aspects of health — from energy and metabolism to immunity and mental clarity. Supporting your microbiome creates positive ripple effects across multiple systems.
goal_cards.formula_link: Your formula is designed holistically — the probiotic mix, prebiotic blend, and targeted nutrients all work together to create the conditions your gut needs to function at its best.

cited_papers.title: Chronic Psychosocial Stress and Gut Health in Children: Associations With Calprotectin and Fecal Short-Chain Fatty Acids
cited_papers.abstract: Objectives The mechanisms underpinning the association between chronic stress and gut health are poorly understood. We aimed to investigate the relationship between bacterial produced short-chain fatty acids, gut barrier function, and stress measures. Methods A fecal sample, hair sample, and questionnaire data were collected from 113 Belgian children (8–16 years old). Biological measures of stress included hair cortisol (most proximal 3 cm) and 5-minute heart rate variability (high frequency). Self-report measures of stress included emotional problems and negative events. Fecal calprotectin was determined as a marker of intestinal inflammation and an indirect indicator of gut barrier integrity. Fecal short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate, acetate, valerate, isobutyrate, and isovale

cited_papers.title: Short‐chain fatty acids: microbial metabolites that alleviate stress‐induced brain–gut axis alterations
cited_papers.abstract: Chronic (psychosocial) stress changes gut microbiota composition, as well as inducing behavioural and physiological deficits. The microbial metabolites short‐chain fatty acids (SCFAs) have been implicated in gastrointestinal functional, (neuro)immune regulation and host metabolism, but their role in stress‐induced behavioural and physiological alterations is poorly understood. Administration of SCFAs to mice undergoing psychosocial stress alleviates enduring alterations in anhedonia and heightened stress‐responsiveness, as well as stress‐induced increases in intestinal permeability. In contrast, chronic stress‐induced alterations in body weight gain, faecal SCFAs and the gene expression of the SCFA receptors FFAR2 and FFAR3 remained unaffected by SCFA supplementation. These results present

cited_papers.title: Does Exercise Alter Gut Microbial Composition? A Systematic Review
cited_papers.abstract: Purpose The objective of this systematic review of literature was to evaluate and summarize published research that has investigated the association between exercise and gut microbial composition in mammals. Methods This review was conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses guidelines. The databases searched for this review included: PubMed; PubMed Central; Medline; Cumulative Index of Nursing and Allied Health Literature; Web of Science; Commonwealth Agricultural Bureaux Direct; Health Source: Nursing Academic Edition; Clinicaltrials.gov; International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO); and the Cochrane Library. Results Twenty-five articles met the inclusion criteria: 17 rodent, one canine, two equine, and five human s

cited_papers.title: Tree-based Analysis of Dietary Diversity Captures Associations between Fiber Intake and Gut Microbiota Composition in a Healthy U.S. Adult Cohort.
cited_papers.abstract:
BACKGROUND
Diet patterns are a significant and modifiable contributing factor to the composition of the human gut microbiota.


OBJECTIVE
We set out to identify reproducible relationships between diet and gut microbial community composition in a diverse, healthy U.S. adult cohort.


METHODS
Two to three Automated Self-Administered 24-hour Dietary Recalls (ASA24) were collected over 10-14 d together with a single stool sample from 343 healthy adults in a cross-sectional phenotyping study (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT02367287). This study examined a multi-ethnic cohort balanced for age (18y-65y), sex, and BMI (18.5-45). Dietary data was edited to a tree format according to published methods. The tree structure was annotated with the average total grams of dry weight, fat, protein, carb
